title: Your Period Tracker Is (Probably) Spying on You
gist: Wired's weekly security roundup: a Mozilla/Harvard audit finds most period-tracker apps leak reproductive health data to ad-tech, Anthropic lobbies states for tougher AI transparency rules, Western governments pin a near-blackout Polish grid attack on Russia's FSB, DHS twice missed a real breach as a false positive, and a hack of AI music startup Suno exposes internal evidence of mass unlicensed scraping.

insights.claim: A Mozilla Foundation audit (with Harvard's Berkman Klein Center) scored the astrology-themed period tracker Stardust 2 out of 10 for privacy, the worst of six popular trackers tested, finding it sends reproductive health details like birth control type, pregnancy status, and symptoms to a data firm not disclosed in its privacy policy.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Consumer health apps with weak data governance carry real regulatory and reputational risk; investors/operators in femtech should audit third-party data-sharing before it surfaces in press or litigation.
insights.quote: Stardust scored 2 out of 10, the worst of the group.
insights.locator: Mozilla Graded Period Trackers on Privacy

insights.claim: Stardust pings third-party analytics firm RudderStack from the moment the app opens (before any user input), attaches a persistent user ID to logged symptoms, and sends Facebook an ad identifier linking in-app health behavior to users' existing ad profiles, with no in-app way to disable the sharing.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Shows a concrete default data pipeline from a health app into ad-tech; a template for the kind of undisclosed data flow that draws regulatory and class-action attention.
insights.locator: Mozilla researcher Shoshana Wodinsky found the app pings third-party trackers

insights.claim: Euki, a nonprofit-run period tracker, scored a perfect 10 in the same audit by requiring no account, keeping health data on-device only, and offering a PIN, auto-deletion, and a decoy screen, showing privacy-by-design is achievable in the category without ad-based monetization.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Sets a public benchmark competitors and regulators can point to, raising pressure on ad-supported period trackers to change data practices or lose users/goodwill.
insights.locator: Euki, a nonprofit-run tracker, earned a perfect 10

insights.claim: Anthropic is actively lobbying US states to pass and expand AI transparency regulation beyond the 2025 California and New York laws, with its head of US state and local government relations arguing that policy must keep pace as AI capabilities advance quickly.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Anthropic is positioning itself as a pro-regulation actor at the state level, which could shape compliance costs and competitive moats for AI labs; watch state legislatures for new transparency bills modeled on CA/NY.
insights.quote: The transparency-focused safety bills of 2025 were a really important start, but as the capabilities of AI systems continue to advance quickly—the policy responses need to match.
insights.locator: Anthropic continued a push to get US states to regulate AI

insights.claim: The EU, UK, and US (via CISA, FBI, and NSA advisories) formally attributed a cyberattack that came "very close" to causing a blackout in Poland's electric and water utilities to Center 16 of Russia's FSB, overturning cybersecurity firms Dragos and ESET's initial attribution to GRU-linked Sandworm (Unit 74455).
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Suggests the FSB is adopting GRU-style disruptive, near-destructive cyber operations rather than pure espionage, raising the threat profile for European critical infrastructure and utilities.
insights.locator: Russia's FSB Sanctioned for Cyberattack on Polish Infrastructure

insights.claim: Denis Obrezko, a Russian man facing US hacking charges for alleged membership in the Void Blizzard/Laundry Bear group that breached NATO governments and at least 11 US companies, spent two years working at Kaspersky before joining Yutek-NN and allegedly took part in the campaign, having reportedly worked at the FSB before Kaspersky.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Adds concrete personnel-level evidence to long-standing (but hard-to-prove) allegations of Kaspersky-Russian government ties, reinforcing the case for continued Western restrictions on Kaspersky products.
insights.quote: the offenses charged cannot be related to the individual's role or responsibilities during the employment at Kaspersky
insights.locator: An Alleged Russian State-Sponsored Hacker Worked for Kaspersky

insights.claim: DHS's Homeland Security Information Network (HSIN) was breached by hackers starting mid-May, but FEMA analysts twice dismissed clear signs of intrusion (altered files/code, a hijacked web server, deleted logs) as false positives before the hackers returned repeatedly.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Points to systemic weakness in federal threat-detection processes against 'living off the land' techniques that abuse legitimate network features instead of deploying detectable malware; relevant to demand for gov cybersecurity vendors and oversight.
insights.locator: A Real DHS Breach Was Twice Ruled a False Positive

insights.claim: A hack of AI music startup Suno exposed internal 2023-2024 source-code notes showing it scraped 113,879 hours of YouTube Music audio plus tens of thousands more hours from Pond5, Deezer, and other stock-audio libraries, and roughly 1 million hours of podcasts via PodcastIndex, routing its YouTube scraping through Bright Data proxies.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Directly corroborates the record industry's core allegation that Suno pulled copyrighted songs from YouTube, strengthening labels' litigation/settlement leverage and raising training-data liability risk for AI music startups.
insights.locator: Hack Exposes an AI Music Generator's Scraping Secrets

insights.claim: The Suno breach also exposed account data for hundreds of thousands of customers, including emails, phone numbers, and Stripe payment records; Suno attributed the intrusion to outdated code and said no sensitive personal information was exposed, but affected customers said they were never notified.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Creates potential breach-notification liability exposure independent of the copyright issue; watch for regulatory action or customer litigation over the lack of notification.
insights.locator: The intrusion also exposed account information for hundreds of thousands of customers

insights.claim: Suno settled a copyright lawsuit with Warner Music Group in November while maintaining its training practices qualify as fair use, but the leaked internal files appear to directly corroborate the labels' central allegation that Suno pulled songs straight from YouTube rather than licensed sources.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Weakens the fair-use defense narrative for AI music generators broadly once internal scraping logs leak, a risk template for other generative-audio startups facing label litigation.
insights.locator: settled with Warner Music Group last November

insights.claim: Hours of San Francisco Police Department drone video footage were exposed on the open web, an operational-security failure the author frames as illustrating a new era of granular urban surveillance with consequential exposure risk.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Highlights a data-security gap in municipal surveillance programs, relevant to vendors and cities deploying drone/camera surveillance without adequate access controls.
insights.locator: Hours of San Francisco Police Department drone video footage exposed

insights.claim: The San Francisco City Attorney's Office sent cease-and-desist letters to Apple and Google demanding removal of 13 AI 'nudifying' face-swap apps from their app stores, apps the office says are used almost exclusively to target women and girls.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Signals growing municipal and legal liability for app-store gatekeepers hosting generative-AI abuse tools, a precedent that could push Apple/Google toward stricter genAI app-review policies.
insights.locator: cease-and-desist letters to Apple and Google

insights.claim: Meta has given opaque and conflicting statements about whether its NameTag face-recognition system for smart glasses actually exists, following Wired's June report and a subsequent report that Meta deleted face-recognition code from its smart-glasses app.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Signals reputational and regulatory overhang for Meta's wearables/AI hardware line from undisclosed or inconsistently described facial-recognition capabilities.
insights.locator: made opaque and conflicting comments about whether the feature even exists

insights.claim: President Trump promised 'massive revelations' in documents posted to the White House website to substantiate unsubstantiated 2020 election-interference claims, but the released files did not prove his assertions and in some cases contradicted them.
insights.speaker: Andy Greenberg
insights.so_what: Illustrates continued information-integrity and credibility risk around official government document releases relevant to political-risk and disinformation tracking.
insights.locator: the files did not prove his assertions
